Keywords: benchmark, peer, comparison, best-practice, gap-analysis, industry
Description: Compare performance against peers or best-in-class. Identifies gaps and sets realistic targets based on what others achieve.
When to Use: When measuring relative performance; Setting targets; Identifying improvement areas; Understanding competitive position; Validating assumptions about what is achievable
How to Apply: Identify comparison set (peers, best-in-class, aspirational); Define metrics to compare; Gather data; Calculate gaps; Identify transferable practices
Data Requirements: Industry databases; Public financial data; Expert networks; Trade publications; Survey data
Output Format: Gap analysis table; Performance radar chart; Best practice inventory; Target recommendations
Strengths: Grounding in reality; External perspective; Objective comparison; Sets achievable targets
Limitations: Past performance doesnt guarantee future; Apples-to-oranges comparisons; Best practices may not transfer; Data availability varies

Description: Ask 'Why?' repeatedly (typically 5 times) to drill below symptoms to the fundamental root cause. Simple but powerful diagnostic technique.
When to Use: When something went wrong; Defect investigation; Process failures; When the obvious explanation seems insufficient; Quick causal analysis
How to Apply: State the problem; Ask 'Why does this happen?'; For the answer, ask 'Why?' again; Repeat 5 times or until root cause reached; Verify root cause by testing: if you fix it, does the problem go away?
Data Requirements: Observational data; Process knowledge; Incident reports; Expert input
Output Format: Causal chain diagram; Root cause identification; Verification test proposal
Strengths: Simple; Fast; Gets below symptoms; No special tools needed; Engages team thinking
Limitations: Can oversimplify (single causal chain); Depends on who is asking; May miss multiple root causes; Subjective; Not suitable for complex systemic issues

Keywords: regression, correlation, prediction, modeling, statistical, relationship, factors
Description: Statistical method to identify relationships between variables and quantify their effects. Distinguishes signal from noise.
When to Use: When you have numerical data; Need to isolate factor effects; Prediction; Testing if a relationship is real; Quantifying impact of individual variables
How to Apply: Identify dependent variable; Select independent variables; Run regression; Evaluate R-squared, coefficients, significance; Validate with holdout data
Data Requirements: Historical numerical data; Sufficient sample size; Independent and dependent variables clearly defined
Output Format: Coefficient table; R-squared; Prediction equation; Significance levels; Residual plots
Strengths: Quantitative; Reproducible; Isolates individual effects; Can test statistical significance
Limitations: Correlation is not causation; Requires good data; Sensitive to outliers; Assumes relationships are stable; Can be misinterpreted

Keywords: scenario, future, uncertainty, planning, resilience, strategic, foresight
Description: Develop multiple plausible futures to stress-test strategies and decisions. Focuses on structural uncertainties rather than single-point forecasts.
When to Use: High uncertainty environments; Long-term strategy; When prediction is unreliable; Investment decisions; Policy planning; When stakes are high
How to Apply: Identify key uncertainties (not risks); Build 2x2 from top 2 uncertainties; Name and develop each quadrant narrative; Test strategy under each scenario; Identify robust moves and contingent moves
Data Requirements: Industry trends; Expert opinions; Historical analogies; Uncertainty assessment; Stakeholder perspectives
Output Format: 2x2 scenario grid with narratives; Strategy robustness assessment; Contingency triggers; No-regret moves list
Strengths: Embraces uncertainty; Prepares for multiple futures; Identifies robust strategies; Challenges linear thinking
Limitations: Scenarios can bias toward comfortable futures; Hard to maintain more than 4; Can become an exercise without action; Probabilities are subjective

Keywords: market-size, fermi, top-down, bottom-up, estimation, TAM, SAM, SOM
Description: Estimate unknown quantities by breaking them into smaller, estimable components. Use both top-down and bottom-up approaches and triangulate.
When to Use: Market entry analysis; Business case building; Quick estimation; When exact data is unavailable; Plausibility checking; Resource planning
How to Apply: Define what you are sizing; Top-down: start from total market, apply successive filters; Bottom-up: estimate unit economics and scale up; Cross-check approaches; Express as range
Data Requirements: Population data; Industry reports; Per-capita statistics; Analogous markets; Unit economics
Output Format: Size estimate with range; Assumption list; Sensitivity to key assumptions; Comparison point (sanity check)
Strengths: Quick; Can be done with limited data; Forces structured thinking; Reveals key assumptions
Limitations: Precision is limited; Sensitive to assumptions; Garbage in garbage out; Easy to anchor on round numbers

Keywords: experiment, test, hypothesis, control, treatment, statistical-significance, causal
Description: Controlled experiment comparing two variants to determine which performs better. Gold standard for causal inference.
When to Use: When you can run experiments; Product feature testing; Pricing tests; Marketing message testing; When you need causal (not just correlational) evidence
How to Apply: Define hypothesis; Design control and treatment; Calculate required sample size; Run experiment; Measure outcome; Check statistical significance; Decide
Data Requirements: Ability to randomize; Sufficient sample size; Clear outcome metric; Enough time for results
Output Format: Statistical comparison; Confidence interval; Effect size; Recommendation with confidence level
Strengths: Causal evidence; Reproducible; Quantitative; Removes opinion from decision
Limitations: Requires time; May not be ethical/feasible in all cases; External validity questions; Novelty effects; Sample size requirements

Keywords: stakeholder, influence, interest, alignment, power, politics, mapping
Description: Map stakeholders by their interest in and influence over the decision. Helps anticipate reactions and build coalitions.
When to Use: Any decision requiring buy-in; Organizational change; Project planning; When politics matter; Before presenting recommendations; Partnership evaluation
How to Apply: List all stakeholders; Rate each on Interest (high/low) and Influence (high/low); Place on 2x2; Develop engagement strategy per quadrant: Manage closely, Keep satisfied, Keep informed, Monitor
Data Requirements: Organizational knowledge; Interview data; Relationship maps; Past decision patterns
Output Format: Stakeholder map (2x2); Engagement strategy table; Coalition plan; Risk mitigation for opponents
Strengths: Anticipates resistance; Builds coalitions; Practical; Focuses communication effort
Limitations: Subjective assessments; Dynamic (changes over time); Can feel manipulative if done poorly; May miss informal influence

Keywords: simulation, probability, distribution, risk, uncertainty, random, range
Description: Run thousands of simulations with random inputs drawn from probability distributions. Produces range of outcomes with probabilities rather than single-point estimates.
When to Use: Investment analysis under uncertainty; Project planning with variable durations; Risk quantification; When multiple uncertain factors interact; Portfolio analysis
How to Apply: Define model with uncertain inputs; Assign probability distributions to each input; Run 1000-10000 simulations; Analyze output distribution; Report confidence intervals and probabilities
Data Requirements: Model structure; Probability distributions for inputs; Correlation assumptions; Computational tool (Excel, Python, R)
Output Format: Probability distribution of outcomes; Confidence intervals; Sensitivity analysis; Risk profile chart
Strengths: Captures full range of outcomes; Shows probability of different results; Handles complex interactions; Visual
Limitations: Requires distributional assumptions; Complex to explain; Can create false precision; Garbage in garbage out; Computational overhead

Keywords: expert, interview, delphi, judgment, elicitation, consensus, opinion
Description: Systematically gather and synthesize expert opinions. Delphi method uses multiple rounds with anonymized feedback to build convergence.
When to Use: When quantitative data is scarce; Novel situations without historical precedent; Need domain expertise; Calibrating assumptions; Understanding complex systems
How to Apply: Identify experts; Prepare structured questions; Conduct interviews (individual or Delphi rounds); Synthesize findings; Look for consensus and divergence; Use to calibrate quantitative models
Data Requirements: Access to experts; Well-structured interview guide; Diverse expert panel; Anonymization mechanism (for Delphi)
Output Format: Interview summary matrix; Consensus/divergence map; Calibrated assumptions; Qualitative insights inventory
Strengths: Captures tacit knowledge; Handles novel situations; Reduces individual bias (Delphi); Rich qualitative data
Limitations: Expert availability; Potential groupthink; Authority bias; Time-intensive; Subjectivity in synthesis

Keywords: pre-mortem, failure, risk, anticipation, prevention, red-team, devils-advocate
Description: Imagine the project has failed and work backward to identify what went wrong. More effective than asking 'what could go wrong' because it gives psychological permission to be critical.
When to Use: Before launching a project; Before making a major decision; When team is too optimistic; Risk assessment; When you need to challenge groupthink
How to Apply: Announce 'the project has failed spectacularly'; Ask each person to write (individually) what went wrong; Share and compile all failure reasons; Group into themes; Develop mitigation plans for most likely/impactful failures
Data Requirements: Team workshop setting; Project plan; Individual writing time; Facilitation skills
Output Format: Failure mode inventory; Risk-mitigation action plan; Updated project plan with contingencies; Watch-list of key risks
Strengths: Powerful psychologically; Surfaces concerns people are reluctant to raise; Practical mitigations; Fast
Limitations: Best done in groups; Depends on team willingness to engage; Can be depressing if not followed by mitigations; May miss truly novel risks

Keywords: analogy, precedent, similar, parallel, case-study, pattern-matching, transfer
Description: Draw insights from similar situations in different domains. Find patterns that transfer. Powerful when direct data is unavailable.
When to Use: Novel situations; When entering unfamiliar territory; Innovation; Strategy development; When direct analogies exist in other industries; Enhancing creativity
How to Apply: Identify the core structural features of your problem; Search for analogous situations (same industry, different industry, different era); Map structural similarities; Extract applicable insights; Test for false analogies
Data Requirements: Case studies; Industry histories; Cross-domain knowledge; Pattern recognition skills
Output Format: Analogy comparison matrix; Transferable insights list; Implementation adaptation plan; False analogy risks
Strengths: Powerful when data is scarce; Cross-pollination of ideas; Helps communicate complex ideas; Accelerates learning from others
Limitations: False analogies (surface similarity without structural match); Anchoring too heavily on one analogy; Ignoring contextual differences; Cherry-picking favorable analogies

Keywords: cost, benefit, NPV, ROI, economic, trade-off, value, investment
Description: Quantify and compare all costs and benefits of a decision in monetary terms. Includes tangible and intangible factors. Standard framework for investment decisions.
When to Use: Investment decisions; Policy evaluation; Project approval; Build vs buy decisions; Feature prioritization; Any decision with quantifiable costs and benefits
How to Apply: List all costs (one-time and ongoing); List all benefits (one-time and ongoing); Convert to present value using discount rate; Calculate net benefit; Test sensitivity to key assumptions; Include qualitative factors
Data Requirements: Financial data; Market data; Discount rate; Timeline estimates; Probability estimates for uncertain items
Output Format: Cost-benefit comparison table; NPV calculation; Sensitivity analysis; Recommendation with payback period
Strengths: Structured; Comparable; Forces quantification; Standard business language
Limitations: Hard-to-quantify benefits get underweighted; Discount rate choice matters enormously; Can create false precision; Dynamic effects ignored
